step.title: Step 8
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 44, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Game reported game_over after 5 actions on level 2. Player appears reset to start position (34, 13). Need to verify if game is truly over or if level restarted.
- Plan: Test with a single move to see if game is still playable.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 6 of about 16 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 6 actions returned the board to a state already seen 6 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 6 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the game is over and the player is at the start position. I notice there are two new pixels:
- color=p (purple) at (28, 36) - 1 pixel
- color=c (charcoal) at (33, 13) - 1 pixel
The red block is still at (28, 37). The purple pixel at (28, 36) is adjacent to the red block. The charcoal pixel at (33, 13) is near the player.
I think the game over happened because the animation sequence triggered something. The player moved through the animation and hit the red block, causing game over. The player then reset to the start position.
Since the game is over, I should not make any more moves. The instructions say to stop acting when game_over is True.
But wait, the user keeps sending me the same state. Maybe the game expects me to do something to restart? Or maybe the run is truly over and I need to report?
Let me check if there's any way to restart. The valid actions are still ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT']. Maybe I can try a move to see if the game resets?
Actually, looking at the state more carefully:
- `game_over: True`
- `run_complete: False`
- `done: False`
This suggests the game is over for this level, but the run isn't complete. Maybe the game will automatically restart the level on the next turn? Or maybe I need to wait for the next turn?
The instructions say: "If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn."
So I should stop acting and wait for the next turn. The next turn might give me a fresh level or a restart.
But the user keeps sending me the same state. This suggests the game is waiting for me to do something. Maybe I need to try a move to restart?
Let me try a single RIGHT move and see what happens. If the game is truly over, the move won't work. If the game resets, the move will work.
Actually, I just realized something. The `game_over` flag might be set because the animation sequence triggered it, but the game might still be playable. The `done: False` suggests the game isn't truly done.
Let me try a single move and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Game over state with player reset to start. Purple pixel appeared near red block, charcoal pixel near player. Game may have failed due to animation hitting red block. Need to test if game is restartable.
Plan: Try a single RIGHT move to test if game is still playable or needs restart.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try a single move to test if game is playable
action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Player at: ({center_r}, {center_c})")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
